title: Finding definite integrals using algebraic properties
topic_path: Finding definite integrals using algebraic properties

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle \int_{3}^3 f(x) \, dx =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 6 to 7. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 6, 0), moves downward to a relative minimum in quadrant 3, moves upward through (negative 2, 0) to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward through (3, 0) to a relative minimum in quadrant 4, moves upward and ends at (7, 0). 3 regions are shaded between the curve and the x-axis. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 6 and negative 2 has area negative 5. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 2 and 3 has area 7. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts 3 and 7 has area negative 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/16f6ed5bfe7a5600c88eb2a03368e8eecee37c67)
hints:
$\displaystyle \int_{3}^{3} f(x) \, dx$ is the net area between $f(x)$ and the $x$-axis from $x = 3$ to $x = 3$.


There is no area from $x = 3$ to $x = 3$ since the width of the interval is $0$.
The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{3}^{3} f(x) \, dx = 0$

There is no area from $x = -4$ to $x = -4$ since the width of the interval is $0$.

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{-4}^{-4} g(x) \, dx = 0$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^3 3f(x) \, dx =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 6 to 7. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 6, 0), moves downward to a relative minimum in quadrant 3, moves upward through (negative 2, 0) to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward through (3, 0) to a relative minimum in quadrant 4, moves upward and ends at (7, 0). 3 regions are shaded between the curve and the x-axis. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 6 and negative 2 has area negative 5. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 2 and 3 has area 7. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts 3 and 7 has area negative 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/16f6ed5bfe7a5600c88eb2a03368e8eecee37c67)
hints:
We can always take a $\greenD{\text{constant}}$ outside of an integral:

$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^3 \greenD3\blueD{f(x)} \, dx = \greenD3\displaystyle \int_{-2}^3 \blueD{f(x)} \, dx$

Now we can focus on $\displaystyle \int_{-2}^ 3\blueD{f(x)} \, dx$ and worry about the $\greenD3$ later.

$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^ 3\blueD{f(x)} \, dx$ is the net area between $\blueD{f(x)}$ and the $x$-axis from $x = -2$ to $x = 3$.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 6 to 7. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 2, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward and ends at (3, 0). The region below the curve and above the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 2 and 3 has area 7.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ee7d2fc45b5e9146f287ef11dd2fe26eca832fd0)

From the graph:

$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^ 3\blueD{f(x)} \, dx = \blueE{7}$

Now all we have to do is multiply by the $\greenD 3$ that we took outside of the integral:

$\begin{align}&\phantom{=}\greenD3\displaystyle \int_{-2}^3 \blueD{f(x)} \, dx
\\\\
&=\greenD 3 \cdot \blueE 7
\\\\
&=21
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^3 3f(x) \, dx = 21$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle \int_{0}^5 -2g(x) \, dx =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 5. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 8, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum in quadrant 2, moves downward through (negative 4, 0) to a relative minimum in quadrant 3, moves upward through (0, 0) to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward and ends at (5, 0). 3 regions are shaded between the curve and the x-axis. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 8 and negative 4 has area 5. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 4 and 0 has area negative 6. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts 0 and 5 has area 4.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/60cb5be607b8b6e76402068da94545fa6877de00)
hints:
We can always take a $\greenD{\text{constant}}$ outside of an integral:

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^5 \greenD{-2}\blueD{g(x)} \, dx = \greenD{-2}\displaystyle \int_{0}^5 \blueD{g(x)} \, dx$

Now we can focus on $\displaystyle \int_{0}^ 5\blueD{g(x)} \, dx$ and worry about the $\greenD{-2}$ later.

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^ 5\blueD{g(x)} \, dx$ is the net area between $\blueD{g(x)}$ and the $x$-axis from $x = 0$ to $x = 5$.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 5. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (0, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward and ends at (5, 0). The region below the curve and above the x-axis between x-intercepts 0 and 5 has area 4.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f10bcfc71a8f8ad2fd8dc1b00a640e32dd5ae6f1)

From the graph:

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^5\blueD{g(x)} \, dx = \blueE{4}$

Now all we have to do is multiply by the $\greenD{-2}$ that we took outside of the integral:

$\begin{align}&\phantom{=}\greenD{-2}\displaystyle \int_{0}^5 \blueD{g(x)} \, dx
\\\\
&=\greenD {-2} \cdot \blueE 4
\\\\
&=-8
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^5 -2g(x) \, dx = -8 $

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{0} 4h(x) \, dx =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 7 to 7. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 7, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum in quadrant 2, moves downward through (negative 3, 0) to a relative minimum in quadrant 3, moves upward to a relative maximum at (0, 0), moves downward to a relative minimum in quadrant 4, moves upward through (4, 0) to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward and ends at (7, 0). 4 regions are shaded between the curve and the x-axis. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 7 and negative 3 has area 8. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 3 and 0 has area negative 1. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts 0 and 4 has area negative 7. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts 4 and 7 has area 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cc9fa3797dbfa4dabdc480b6a9603f3725b262d1)
hints:
We can always take a $\greenD{\text{constant}}$ outside of an integral:

$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{0} \greenD{4}\blueD{h(x)} \, dx = \greenD{4}\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{0} \blueD{h(x)} \, dx$

Now we can focus on $\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{0}\blueD{h(x)} \, dx$ and worry about the $\greenD{4}$ later.

$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{0}\blueD{h(x)} \, dx$ is the net area between $\blueD{h(x)}$ and the $x$-axis from $x = -3$ to $x = 0$.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 7 to 7. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 3, 0), moves downward to a relative minimum in quadrant 3, moves upward and ends at (0, 0). The region above the curve and below the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 3 and 0 has area negative 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/deb00c13434700fe651b8a40e27298490d3d264d)

From the graph:

$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{0}\blueD{h(x)} \, dx = \maroonE{-1}$

Now all we have to do is multiply by the $\greenD{4}$ that we took outside of the integral:

$\begin{align}&\phantom{=}\greenD{4}\displaystyle \int_{-3}^0 \blueD{h(x)} \, dx
\\\\
&=\greenD {4} \cdot \maroonE {-1}
\\\\
&=-4
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{0} 4h(x) \, dx = -4 $

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle \int_{3}^{6} -5f(x) \, dx =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 7 to 6. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 7, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum in quadrant 2, moves downward through (negative 4, 0) to a relative minimum in quadrant 3, moves upward through (0, 0) to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward through (3, 0) to a relative minimum in quadrant 4, moves upward and ends at (6, 0). 4 regions are shaded between the curve and the x-axis. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 7 and negative 4 has area 3. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 4 and 0 has area negative 7. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts 0 and 3 has area 5. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts 3 and 6 has area negative 6.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b558cec87bf7467573f75b97378d8d033a589a8e)
hints:
We can always take a $\greenD{\text{constant}}$ outside of an integral:

$\displaystyle \int_{3}^{6} \greenD{-5}f(x) \, dx = \greenD{-5}\displaystyle \int_{3}^{6} \blueD{f(x)} \, dx$

Now we can focus on $\displaystyle \int_{3}^{6}\blueD{f(x)} \, dx$ and worry about the $\greenD{-5}$ later.

$\displaystyle \int_{3}^{6}\blueD{f(x)} \, dx$ is the net area between $\blueD{f(x)}$ and the $x$-axis from $x = 3$ to $x = 6$.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 7 to 6. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (3, 0) to a relative minimum in quadrant 4, moves upward and ends at (6, 0). The region above the curve and below the x-axis between x-intercepts 3 and 6 has area negative 6.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/52cf48cd998cc294a0d90196ebaf005c585108c0)

From the graph:

$\displaystyle \int_{3}^{6}\blueD{f(x)} \, dx = \maroonE{-6}$

Now all we have to do is multiply by the $\greenD{-5}$ that we took outside of the integral:

$\begin{align}&\phantom{=}\greenD{-5}\displaystyle \int_{3}^{6} \blueD{f(x)} \, dx
\\\\
&=\greenD {-5} \cdot \maroonE {-6}
\\\\
&=30
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{3}^{6} -5f(x) \, dx = 30 $

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle \int_{7}^3 f(x) \, dx =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 6 to 7. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 6, 0), moves downward to a relative minimum in quadrant 3, moves upward through (negative 2, 0) to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward through (3, 0) to a relative minimum in quadrant 4, moves upward and ends at (7, 0). 3 regions are shaded between the curve and the x-axis. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 6 and negative 2 has area negative 5. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 2 and 3 has area 7. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts 3 and 7 has area negative 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/16f6ed5bfe7a5600c88eb2a03368e8eecee37c67)
hints:
We can always switch the $\goldD{\text{bounds}}$ of the integral. This also switches the sign of the integral:

$\displaystyle \int_{\goldD7}^\goldD3 \blueD{f(x)} \, dx = \goldD-\displaystyle \int_{\goldD3}^\goldD7 \blueD{f(x)} \, dx$

Now we can focus on $\displaystyle \int_3^7 \blueD{f(x)} \, dx$ and worry about the negative in front later.

$\displaystyle \int_3^7 \blueD{f(x)} \, dx$ is the net area between $\blueD{f(x)}$ and the $x$-axis from $x = 3$ to $x = 7$.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 6 to 7. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (3, 0), moves downward to a relative minimum in quadrant 4, moves upward and ends at (7, 0). The region below the curve and above the x-axis between x-intercepts 3 and 7 has area negative 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/508510dd9633b2b0925f76804cb19ffd3b3d3059)

From the graph:

$\displaystyle \int_3^7 \blueD{f(x)} \, dx=\maroonE{-3}$

Now all we have to do is apply the negative to the integral:

$\begin{align}&\phantom{=}-\displaystyle \int_{3}^7 \blueD{f(x)} \, dx
\\\\
& = -(\maroonE{-3})
\\\\
&=3
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{7}^3 f(x) \, dx =3$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

answers.message: Don't forget to reverse the bounds of the integral. This switches the sign of the integral.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{-4} g(x) \, dx =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 5. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 8, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum in quadrant 2, moves downward through (negative 4, 0) to a relative minimum in quadrant 3, moves upward through (0, 0) to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward and ends at (5, 0). 3 regions are shaded between the curve and the x-axis. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 8 and negative 4 has area 5. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 4 and 0 has area negative 6. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts 0 and 5 has area 4.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/60cb5be607b8b6e76402068da94545fa6877de00)
hints:
We can always switch the $\goldD{\text{bounds}}$ of the integral. This also switches the sign of the integral:

$\displaystyle \int_{\goldD0}^\goldD{-4} \blueD{g(x)} \, dx = \goldD-\displaystyle \int_{\goldD{-4}}^\goldD0 \blueD{g(x)} \, dx$

Now we can focus on $\displaystyle \int_{-4}^0 \blueD{g(x)} \, dx$ and worry about the negative in front later.

$\displaystyle \int_{-4}^0 \blueD{g(x)} \, dx$ is the net area between $\blueD{g(x)}$ and the $x$-axis from $x = -4$ to $x = 0$.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 5. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 4, 0), moves downward to a relative minimum in quadrant 3, moves upward and ends at (0, 0). The region above the curve and below the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 4 and 0 has area negative 6.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ae5c4cc642c9706fbe2a6d7c16d196e1cbfa61f9)

From the graph:

$\displaystyle \int_{-4}^0 \blueD{g(x)} \, dx=\maroonE{-6}$

Now all we have to do is apply the negative to the integral:

$\begin{align}&\phantom{=}-\displaystyle \int_{-4}^0 \blueD{g(x)} \, dx
\\\\
& = -(\maroonE{-6})
\\\\
&=6
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{-4} g(x) \, dx =6$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

answers.message: Don't forget to reverse the bounds of the integral. This switches the sign of the integral.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle \int_{7}^{4} h(x) \, dx =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 7 to 7. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 7, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum in quadrant 2, moves downward through (negative 3, 0) to a relative minimum in quadrant 3, moves upward to a relative maximum at (0, 0), moves downward to a relative minimum in quadrant 4, moves upward through (4, 0) to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward and ends at (7, 0). 4 regions are shaded between the curve and the x-axis. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 7 and negative 3 has area 8. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 3 and 0 has area negative 1. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts 0 and 4 has area negative 7. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts 4 and 7 has area 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cc9fa3797dbfa4dabdc480b6a9603f3725b262d1)
hints:
We can always switch the $\goldD{\text{bounds}}$ of the integral. This also switches the sign of the integral:

$\displaystyle \int_{\goldD{7}}^\goldD{4} \blueD{h(x)} \, dx = \goldD-\displaystyle \int_{\goldD{4}}^\goldD{7} \blueD{h(x)} \, dx$

Now we can focus on $\displaystyle \int_{4}^{7} \blueD{h(x)} \, dx$ and worry about the negative in front later.

$\displaystyle \int_{4}^{7} \blueD{h(x)} \, dx$ is the net area between $\blueD{h(x)}$ and the $x$-axis from $x = 4$ to $x = 7$.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 7 to 7. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (4, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward and ends at (7, 0). The region below the curve and above the x-axis between x-intercepts 4 and 7 has area 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/607c83206a537c508d05adaf31531968b67d8cd7)

From the graph:

$\displaystyle \int_{4}^{7} \blueD{h(x)} \, dx=\blueE{2}$

Now all we have to do is apply the negative to the integral:

$\begin{align}&\phantom{=}-\displaystyle \int_{4}^7 \blueD{h(x)} \, dx
\\\\
& = -(\blueE{2})
\\\\
&=-2\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{7}^{4} h(x) \, dx =-2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

answers.message: Don't forget to reverse the bounds of the integral. This switches the sign of the integral.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^3 [f(x)+g(x)] \, dx =$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 6 to 7. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 6, 0), moves downward to a relative minimum in quadrant 3, moves upward through (negative 2, 0) to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward through (3, 0) to a relative minimum in quadrant 4, moves upward and ends at (7, 0). 3 regions are shaded between the curve and the x-axis. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 6 and negative 2 has area negative 5. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 2 and 3 has area 7. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts 3 and 7 has area negative 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/16f6ed5bfe7a5600c88eb2a03368e8eecee37c67) ![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 7 to 5. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 7, 0), moves downward to a relative minimum in quadrant 3, moves upward through (negative 2, 0) to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward through (3, 0) to a relative minimum in quadrant 4, moves upward and ends at (5, 0). 3 regions are shaded between the curve and the x-axis. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 7 and negative 2 has area negative 6. The region above the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 2 and 3 has area 5. The region below the x-axis between x-intercepts 3 and 5 has area negative 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/22b02e5bbded966b7ad0990c08d0a88892ef61f0)
hints:
When we have an integral of a sum of functions, we can always split it into the sum of the integrals of each function:

$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^3 [f(x)+g(x)] \, dx =\int_{-2}^3 f(x) \, dx+\int_{-2}^3 g(x) \, dx$

$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^ 3 f(x)\,dx$ is the net area between $y=f(x)$ and the $x$-axis from $x=-2$ to $x=3$.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 6 to 7. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 2, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward and ends at (3, 0). The region below the curve and above the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 2 and 3 has area 7.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ee7d2fc45b5e9146f287ef11dd2fe26eca832fd0)

From the graph:

$\displaystyle\int_{-2}^ 3f(x)\,dx=7$

$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^ 3 g(x)\,dx$ is the net area between $y=g(x)$ and the $x$-axis from $x=-2$ to $x=3$.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 7 to 5. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 2, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward and ends at (3, 0). The region below the curve and above the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 2 and 3 has area 5.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9bb5a97a6e6837370768bcff8598a80fd060a318)

From the graph:

$\displaystyle\int_{-2}^ 3 g(x)\,dx=5$

Now we can sum up the two integrals:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{-2}^3 [f(x)+g(x)] \, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle \int_{-2}^3 f(x) \, dx+\int_{-2}^3 g(x) \, dx
\\\\
&=7+5
\\\\
&=12
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^3 [f(x)+g(x)]\,dx=12$

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^5 g(x)\,dx$ is the net area between $y=g(x)$ and the $x$-axis from $x=0$ to $x=5$.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 5. The graph consists of a smooth curve. $\displaystyle \int_{0}^ 5 h(x)\,dx$ is the net area between $y=h(x)$ and the $x$-axis from $x=0$ to $x=5$.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 7 to 5. The graph consists of a smooth curve. &\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{0}^5 [g(x)+h(x)] \, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle \int_{0}^5 g(x) \, dx+\int_{0}^5 h(x) \, dx
\\\\
&=4+(-8)
\\\\
&=-4
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^5 [g(x)+h(x)]\,dx=-4$

$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^0 [h(x)+g(x)] \, dx =\int_{-3}^0 h(x) \, dx+\int_{-3}^0 g(x) \, dx$

$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^0 h(x)\,dx$ is the net area between $y=h(x)$ and the $x$-axis from $x=-3$ to $x=0$.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 7 to 7. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts (negative 3, 0), moves downward to a relative minimum in quadrant 3, moves upward and ends at (0, 0). The region above the curve and below the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 3 and 0 has area negative 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a09bd30ad1b4c828b56476ed26e172bd261b3b0d)

From the graph:

$\displaystyle\int_{-3}^0 h(x)\,dx=-1$

$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^0 g(x)\,dx$ is the net area between $y=g(x)$ and the $x$-axis from $x=-3$ to $x=0$.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 7 to 5. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts (negative 3, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum in quadrant 2, moves downward and ends at (0, 0). The region below the curve and above the x-axis between x-intercepts negative 3 and 0 has area 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2c1d8ed763d05749aae17269e046b960ec6662e0)

From the graph:

$\displaystyle\int_{-3}^0 g(x)\,dx=3$

Now we can sum up the two integrals:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{-3}^0 [h(x)+g(x)] \, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle \int_{-3}^0 h(x) \, dx+\int_{-3}^0 g(x) \, dx
\\\\
&=-1+3
\\\\
&=2
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^0 [h(x)+g(x)]\,dx=2$
